title: 4:3 Aspect Ratio Calculator - Traditional Display Dimensions
tagline: Calculate width, height, diagonal size, pixel density, and area for 4:3 aspect ratio displays used in classic monitors, legacy systems, and retro applications.
intro: Enter a width or height to calculate the matching 4:3 dimension. Optionally add a diagonal measurement to calculate pixel density.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid positive number for width or height.
error.noInput: Enter at least a width or height value to calculate.

result.heading: 4:3 Dimensions
result.noPpi: Enter diagonal size to calculate PPI

about.heading: About the 4:3 aspect ratio calculator
about.body:
The 4:3 aspect ratio is defined by a simple mathematical relationship: width divided by height equals 1.333... (four-thirds). For every four units of width, the display is exactly three units tall. This ratio produces a nearly square screen format that was dominant in computing and television for most of the 20th century, from the earliest cathode-ray tube monitors through the PC era of the 1990s and early 2000s.

Understanding the 4:3 aspect ratio begins with the ratio itself. The fraction 4/3 equals approximately 1.3333 repeating, meaning the display is about 33% wider than it is tall. This contrasts with today's ubiquitous 16:9 widescreen format, which is 78% wider than tall (16/9 ≈ 1.778). The 4:3 format feels more "boxy" or "square" to modern viewers accustomed to widescreen content, but it maps naturally to traditional written page proportions and was well-suited to early operating systems and business software.

The most common 4:3 pixel resolutions are 640×480 (VGA), 800×600 (SVGA), 1024×768 (XGA), 1280×960, and 1600×1200 (UXGA). Each of these maintains the exact 4:3 ratio. You can verify any resolution by dividing width by height: 1024/768 = 1.3333..., confirming the ratio. Resolutions like 1280×1024 appear nearly 4:3 but actually have a 5:4 ratio (1.25), which is why these displays can look slightly stretched when displaying content designed for true 4:3.

Physical dimensions matter as much as pixel counts. A 17-inch CRT monitor displaying 1024×768 pixels has a pixel density of approximately 75 pixels per inch (PPI), calculated by dividing the diagonal pixel count by the physical diagonal measurement. The diagonal pixel count for 1024×768 is √(1024² + 768²) = √(1048576 + 589824) = √1638400 = 1280 pixels. Dividing 1280 by 17 inches gives 75.3 PPI, which was the standard for comfortable desktop viewing at arm's length.

The calculator accepts inputs in pixels, inches, centimeters, and millimeters, making it useful for both digital design work and physical display specification. When working with pixels, the aspect ratio calculation is purely mathematical. When working with physical units, the calculator additionally computes the display area, which is valuable for space planning and ergonomics assessments.

The 4:3 format remains relevant today for several specific use cases. Retro gaming and emulation requires accurate 4:3 output to reproduce the authentic look of classic games designed for CRT screens. Industrial and medical equipment often uses legacy 4:3 displays because replacing entire systems is costly and the software is validated for specific hardware configurations. Video conferencing and document cameras frequently use 4:3 framing because it centers the speaker's face and upper body more naturally than widescreen formats. Certain photographic and fine-art print formats also approximate 4:3 proportions, such as the classic 4×5 inch large-format camera negative.

When verifying whether a display truly has a 4:3 ratio, the calculator checks that width/height is within a small tolerance of 1.3333. Minor deviations can occur from rounding in specifications or from non-square pixels in some legacy display technologies. The calculator reports the actual computed ratio so you can judge whether the discrepancy is significant for your application.

examples.heading: 4:3 aspect ratio examples
examples.intro: Common 4:3 display configurations from CRT monitors to tablets, showing real pixel dimensions and physical sizes.

items.note: Classic XGA resolution. At 17 inches diagonal, pixel density ≈ 75 PPI — comfortable for desktop viewing at arm's length.
Same resolution on a smaller screen gives higher pixel density ≈ 91 PPI, with sharper text but smaller on-screen elements.
SVGA resolution on a compact 10-inch tablet. Pixel density ≈ 100 PPI, suitable for web browsing and document reading.
UXGA resolution gives excellent sharpness on legacy professional monitors and is used for high-resolution 4:3 content.

howto.heading: How to use the 4:3 aspect ratio calculator
steps: Select the unit of measurement — pixels for digital design work, inches or centimeters for physical display sizing.
Enter either a width or a height value. The calculator will compute the missing dimension using the 4:3 ratio (height = width × 3/4, or width = height × 4/3).
Optionally enter the physical diagonal size in the same unit. This enables the calculator to determine pixel density (PPI) when working with pixels.
Click Calculate to see the full set of 4:3 dimensions including the complementary dimension, diagonal, area, and aspect ratio verification.
Use the example preset buttons below the results table to instantly load common 4:3 display configurations for reference.

faq.heading: 4:3 aspect ratio calculator FAQ
faq.q1: What is the 4:3 aspect ratio in decimal?
faq.q1_a: The 4:3 aspect ratio equals 1.3333... (one and one-third) as a decimal. You can verify any display by dividing its width by its height — if the result is close to 1.333, the display is 4:3. Common 4:3 resolutions include 640×480, 800×600, 1024×768, and 1600×1200.
faq.q2: How do I find the 4:3 height from a width?
faq.q2_a: Multiply the width by 3/4 (or 0.75). For a width of 1024 pixels, the 4:3 height is 1024 × 0.75 = 768 pixels. Conversely, to find width from height, multiply by 4/3 (or 1.3333). For a height of 600 pixels, the width is 600 × 1.3333 = 800 pixels.
faq.q3: What is the diagonal of a 1024×768 display?
faq.q3_a: Use the Pythagorean theorem: diagonal = √(width² + height²) = √(1024² + 768²) = √(1048576 + 589824) = √1638400 = 1280 pixels (or the equivalent physical measurement). If the monitor is 17 inches diagonal, the PPI is 1280/17 ≈ 75.3.
faq.q4_a: No. 1280×1024 has a 5:4 aspect ratio (1280/1024 = 1.25), not 4:3. It was a common resolution for 17-inch and 19-inch LCD monitors in the early 2000s but content designed for 4:3 will appear slightly stretched vertically or compressed horizontally on these displays without correction.
faq.q5: Why do some retro games look wrong on modern widescreen displays?
faq.q5_a: Games designed for CRT televisions and early computer monitors assumed a 4:3 display. Stretching them to fill a 16:9 or 16:10 widescreen makes objects appear wider than intended. The correct approach is to add black bars (pillarboxing) on the sides to preserve the original 4:3 aspect ratio, or to use an emulator with proper aspect ratio correction.
faq.q6: What physical sizes are common for 4:3 monitors?
faq.q6_a: Common 4:3 CRT and early LCD sizes include 14-inch (1024×768), 15-inch (1024×768 or 1280×960), 17-inch (1280×1024 or 1024×768), and 21-inch (1600×1200). Most modern monitors are widescreen, but 4:3 monitors are still produced for industrial, medical, and retro computing applications.
